title: To Kill a Mockingbird
author: Harper Lee
desc: The defining American novel about racial injustice and moral courage — told through the eyes of young Scout Finch as her father Atticus defends a Black man in 1930s Alabama. Warm, funny and devastating. One of the most readable classics ever written.

title: Things Fall Apart
author: Chinua Achebe
desc: The great African novel. Colonisation arrives in an Igbo village told from the inside — through the proud, flawed warrior Okonkwo. Short, powerful, and will completely shift how you think about empire and civilisation. The most widely read African book in the world.

title: The Kite Runner
author: Khaled Hosseini
desc: A story of friendship, betrayal and redemption set against Afghanistan's history from the 1970s through the Taliban era. Gripping and emotional — gives you an intimate window into a country most of us only know from headlines.

title: A Thousand Splendid Suns
author: Khaled Hosseini
desc: Two Afghan women from different generations, forced together as wives to the same abusive man, find in each other the strength to survive — across decades of war and Taliban rule. Even more devastating than The Kite Runner.

title: Persepolis
author: Marjane Satrapi
desc: A graphic memoir of growing up in Iran during the Islamic Revolution. Satrapi is funny, honest and heartbreaking. Reads in a sitting and teaches you more about Iran than any news report. Named on multiple best-of-century lists.

title: My Brilliant Friend
author: Elena Ferrante
desc: A ferocious, intimate story of female friendship set in a poor Naples neighbourhood from the 1950s. Compulsively readable — named NYT's #1 book of the 21st century. If you read one novel this year, make it this one.

title: Homegoing
author: Yaa Gyasi
desc: Two half-sisters in 18th-century Ghana — one marries a British coloniser, one is sold into slavery — and the novel follows their descendants across 300 years. Each chapter is a different generation. Gripping, heartbreaking, and teaches you everything about the slave trade.

title: Educated
author: Tara Westover
desc: A woman who grew up in a survivalist Idaho family — never attending school — eventually earns a PhD from Cambridge. About family, knowledge and the courage to think for yourself. The most compelling memoir of recent years.

title: Exit West
author: Mohsin Hamid
desc: In a country on the brink of civil war, two people fall in love. Then magic doors appear — stepping through transports refugees across the world. Beautiful, urgent and achingly human. Short and essential reading right now.

title: I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings
author: Maya Angelou
desc: Maya Angelou's memoir of childhood in the American South — racial humiliation, trauma and the transcendence of literature. One of the great American voices, on one of the most important subjects. Beautiful, warm and essential.

title: Pachinko
author: Min Jin Lee
desc: A Korean family across four generations living as outsiders in Japan — from the 1910s occupation to 1980s Osaka. About discrimination, sacrifice and survival. One of the most talked-about novels of the decade. Utterly absorbing.

title: A Gentleman in Moscow
author: Amor Towles
desc: A Russian count sentenced to lifelong house arrest in a grand Moscow hotel in 1922. What unfolds is a warm, witty, beautifully observed novel about how to live with grace. Possibly the most enjoyable literary novel of the 2010s.

title: Never Let Me Go
author: Kazuo Ishiguro
desc: Kathy H looks back on growing up in an idyllic English boarding school. Quietly, slowly, you realise something is deeply wrong. Ishiguro's most haunting novel — about memory, mortality and what it means to be human. Reads in two sittings.

title: The Remains of the Day
author: Kazuo Ishiguro
desc: An English butler takes a road trip and quietly reflects on a life of service, missed chances and unspoken love. Devastating in its restraint. Booker Prize winner. Nobel laureate Ishiguro at his most precise — and most moving.

title: The God of Small Things
author: Arundhati Roy
desc: A gorgeous, devastating novel about family, forbidden love and caste in Kerala. Roy's prose is lush and inventive. The story of twins separated by tragedy. Booker Prize winner. Not difficult reading — just very rich.

title: Americanah
author: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie
desc: Ifemelu leaves Nigeria for America — a love story that becomes a sharp, funny, essential examination of race and identity. What it means to be African in the West, seen from both sides. Adichie is one of the most important writers alive.

title: Half of a Yellow Sun
author: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie
desc: Nigeria in the 1960s as the devastating Biafra war tears the country apart — seen through three intertwined characters. Orange Prize winner. Adichie makes history feel personal and urgent. Essential reading about Africa.

title: The White Tiger
author: Aravind Adiga
desc: Balram Halwai narrates his rise from village poverty to entrepreneurship in Delhi, by any means necessary. Dark, funny and ferociously readable — a portrait of modern India's inequality from the bottom looking up. Booker Prize winner.

title: The Namesake
author: Jhumpa Lahiri
desc: The Ganguli family emigrate from Calcutta to Boston. Their son Gogol grows up caught between two worlds. Lahiri's warm, precise novel about immigration, identity and family. Beautifully easy to read while carrying real depth.

title: Disgrace
author: J.M. Coetzee
desc: A Cape Town professor falls from grace after an affair with a student, then retreats to his daughter's rural farm where something terrible happens. Short, brutal and morally complex — Coetzee's most piercing novel about post-apartheid South Africa.

title: A Passage to India
author: E.M. Forster
desc: A British woman visits colonial India and an accusation explodes across the racial divide. Forster's masterpiece on empire, friendship and cultural misunderstanding is eerily prescient. One of the great novels about colonialism.

title: One Hundred Years of Solitude
author: Gabriel García Márquez
desc: The Buendía family saga through seven generations in the fictional town of Macondo — war, love, magic and madness. The father of magic realism. Nobel Prize winner. More accessible than its reputation suggests — García Márquez is a natural storyteller.

title: The House of the Spirits
author: Isabel Allende
desc: A sweeping family saga across three generations in Chile, ending with the 1973 coup. Magic realism in the García Márquez tradition but with a unique Latin American female voice. Allende's most beloved novel — gripping, romantic and politically essential.

title: The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
author: Junot Díaz
desc: An overweight Dominican-American nerd obsessed with sci-fi tries to find love while his family carries the shadow of Trujillo's dictatorship. Funny, heartbreaking and linguistically electric. Pulitzer Prize winner. A total original.

title: The Shadow of the Wind
author: Carlos Ruiz Zafón
desc: A boy finds a mysterious novel in a secret library and sets out to find its reclusive author — entering a labyrinth of mystery, love and tragedy in Franco's Barcelona. A genuine page-turner that makes you want to visit Barcelona immediately.

title: The Handmaid's Tale
author: Margaret Atwood
desc: In the Republic of Gilead — formerly the USA — women are the property of the state. Offred is a Handmaid assigned to bear children for a Commander. Atwood's chilling masterwork feels more relevant with every passing year.

title: The Vegetarian
author: Han Kang
desc: A South Korean woman decides to stop eating meat, setting off a violent reaction from her family. Short, disturbing and beautifully strange — Han Kang's Man Booker International Prize winner. Her 2024 Nobel Prize made her essential. Start here.

title: Snow
author: Orhan Pamuk
desc: A poet returns to Turkey after years in exile and is snowbound in a remote city at the heart of political and religious conflict. Pamuk's most accessible novel — detective story, love story and political thriller about secular versus Islamic Turkey. Nobel Prize winner.

title: The Reluctant Fundamentalist
author: Mohsin Hamid
desc: A Pakistani man who loved America sits with an American in a Lahore café and tells his story — his rise on Wall Street, then 9/11, then the reckoning. Short, gripping and clever. One of the best post-9/11 novels and essential reading about Pakistan.

title: The Old Man and the Sea
author: Ernest Hemingway
desc: An aged Cuban fisherman heads alone into the deep sea and hooks a great marlin. Hemingway's shortest, most perfect novel. Pulitzer Prize winner. Reads in two hours. About dignity, struggle and ageing — feels more relevant at 42 than at 22.

title: Hamnet
author: Maggie O'Farrell
desc: Plague arrives in 1580s Stratford-upon-Avon and a boy called Hamnet dies. His father is a glover who writes plays. O'Farrell's devastating reimagining of Shakespeare's family and loss. Women's Prize winner. Beautifully written and hard to put down.

title: Lincoln in the Bardo
author: George Saunders
desc: President Lincoln sits with his dead 11-year-old son while ghosts swirl around the Washington cemetery. Told entirely through historical quotes and ghost voices. Booker Prize winner. Surprisingly warm and moving — and genuinely unlike anything else.

title: The Joy Luck Club
author: Amy Tan
desc: Four Chinese immigrant women and their American-born daughters — their secrets, their clashing worlds and what gets lost in translation between generations. Tender, funny and heartbreaking. An ideal entry point into Chinese-American experience.

title: Invisible Man
author: Ralph Ellison
desc: A young Black man narrates his journey from the South to Harlem, discovering that society refuses to see him as a person. One of the most important American novels ever written — about race, identity and invisibility. National Book Award winner.

title: We Should All Be Feminists
author: Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie
desc: Adichie's essential short essay on feminism — personal, funny and clear. Only 64 pages, reads in an hour. Given to every 16-year-old in Sweden. One of the most widely read essays of the 21st century.

title: A Long Way Gone
author: Ishmael Beah
desc: Beah was 12 when Sierra Leone's civil war took his family. By 13 he was a child soldier. This clear-eyed, devastating memoir about how an ordinary boy was turned into a killer — and then rehabilitated — will stay with you permanently.

title: The Glass Palace
author: Amitav Ghosh
desc: Beginning with the fall of Burma's last king in 1885, Ghosh follows three families across a century of colonialism, war and independence across South-East Asia. Epic but readable — teaches you an entire region's history through lived experience.

title: Slaughterhouse-Five
author: Kurt Vonnegut
desc: Billy Pilgrim, a WW2 POW, becomes unstuck in time and keeps revisiting the firebombing of Dresden, his suburban life and an alien planet. One of the great anti-war novels — astonishingly funny. Short, strange and utterly unforgettable.

title: Midnight's Children
author: Salman Rushdie
desc: Born at the moment of Indian independence, Saleem Sinai discovers he has telepathic links to the 1,001 other children born that night. An exhilarating, comic and deeply Indian novel. Booker of Bookers. Rushdie at his most joyful — a bit long but worth it.

title: The Alchemist
author: Paulo Coelho
desc: A short, lyrical fable about a Spanish shepherd boy travelling to Egypt in search of treasure. The world's most translated novel after the Bible. Quick to read, universally loved — about following your dreams and listening to the world around you.

title: Bel Canto
author: Ann Patchett
desc: Terrorists take hostages at a South American dinner party and over weeks, extraordinary bonds form between captors and captives — with an opera singer at the heart of it all. Warm, unexpected and unlike any other novel. PEN/Faulkner Award winner.

title: Kitchen
author: Banana Yoshimoto
desc: Mikage, newly orphaned, is taken in by a boy and his transgender mother and finds solace in kitchens. Two novellas about grief, food and finding home. Gentle, warm and deeply Japanese. Reads in one sitting — one of Japan's most loved exports.

title: Kokoro
author: Natsume Soseki
desc: A young man befriends an enigmatic older man called Sensei who is hiding a secret from his past. Japan's most beloved novel — about guilt, loneliness and the end of the Meiji era. Short, beautiful and strangely modern.

title: The Boat
author: Nam Le
desc: Seven stories spanning continents and cultures — a Vietnamese-Australian with a refugee father, a teenage hitman in Colombia, an elderly painter in Hiroshima. The title story about Vietnamese boat people is unforgettable. An Australian debut of rare ambition.

title: The Swallows of Kabul
author: Yasmina Khadra
desc: Four lives in Taliban-controlled Kabul — two couples whose fates intertwine through a public execution. Short and devastating. One of the most powerful novels about life under the Taliban, written by an Algerian author with extraordinary insight.

title: The Luminaries
author: Eleanor Catton
desc: A murder mystery set on New Zealand's South Island during the 1866 gold rush. Booker Prize winner — the youngest ever at 28. Long but structured by astrology and genuinely thrilling. Will teach you more about New Zealand history than anything else.

title: A Fine Balance
author: Rohinton Mistry
desc: Four strangers thrown together in Bombay during Indira Gandhi's 1975 Emergency. Long but utterly absorbing — one of the great novels about India's poor. Oprah's Book Club pick. Heartbreaking and deeply human.

title: Catch-22
author: Joseph Heller
desc: Captain Yossarian tries to get out of flying WW2 missions by proving he's insane — but wanting to survive proves you're sane. The great American satire on war and bureaucracy. Hilarious and horrifying in equal measure.

title: In the Time of the Butterflies
author: Julia Alvarez
desc: The true story of the four Mirabal sisters who stood up against the Trujillo dictatorship in the Dominican Republic. Beautifully written and compulsively readable — one of the great political novels of the Americas and a story of extraordinary courage.

title: Things We Lost in the Fire
author: Mariana Enriquez
desc: Twelve horror stories set in contemporary Argentina — but the real monsters are poverty, machismo, and the country's brutal history of disappeared people. Dark, original and genuinely frightening. One of the freshest voices in world literature.

title: The Sellout
author: Paul Beatty
desc: A Black man in a fictional LA ghetto reinstates slavery and segregation in his own town. The most savage and funniest satire on race in America ever written. Booker Prize winner — the first American ever to win it. Not easy, but utterly unlike anything else.
